vulnerability.title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
vulnerability.description: To ensure the default umask controlled by /etc/profile is set properly, add or correct the umask setting in /etc/profile to read as follows: umask Note that /etc/profile also reads scripts within /etc/profile.d directory. These scripts are also valid files to set umask value. Therefore, they should also be considered during the check and properly remediated, if necessary.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_accounts_user_umask='' readarray -t profile_files < <(find /etc/profile.d/ -type f -name '*.sh' -or -name 'sh.local') for file in "${profile_files[@]}" /etc/profile; do grep -qE '^[^#]*umask' "$file" && sed -i -E "s/^(\s*umask\s*)[0-7]+/\1$var_accounts_user_umask/g" "$file" done if ! grep -qrE '^[^#]*umask' /etc/profile*; then echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> /etc/profile fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: Setting the default umask in /etc/profile and /etc/profile.d scripts is a standard configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services, and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: Incorrect umask value may lead to overly permissive file creation permissions

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi
umask 022

01-umask.sh
colorgrep.csh
colorgrep.sh
colorls.csh
colorls.sh
colorxzgrep.csh
colorxzgrep.sh
colorzgrep.csh
colorzgrep.sh
csh.local
debuginfod.csh
debuginfod.sh
gawk.csh
gawk.sh
lang.csh
lang.sh
less.csh
less.sh
sh.local
tmout.sh
which2.csh
which2.sh

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else

fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do

done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi

llm_verdict.message: ✅ The required changes have been successfully applied.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan effectively ensures that the default umask is set to 022 across the system‑wide shell initialization files, addressing the vulnerability. The approach is sound and will resolve the issue if executed correctly.
concerns: The sed substitution pattern `s/^(\\\s*umask\\s*)[0-7]+/\\1 022/` may introduce double spaces before the value and will replace *any* numeric value, not just a three‑digit umask, potentially affecting non‑standard lines. The condition for verifying an active umask line uses `grep -qE '^[^#]*umask'`, which matches lines where `umask` appears anywhere after a non‑# character—this could misinterpret comments that contain the word ‘umask’ but are not setting it.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan addresses the vulnerability by ensuring an umask of 022 is set in both /etc/profile and a dedicated /etc/profile.d script with idempotent modifications. It will resolve the issue when executed correctly.
concerns:
Sed substitution pattern does not capture leading whitespace, potentially altering indentation; it replaces any three‑digit number regardless of context.
The approach overwrites /etc/profile.d/umask.sh unconditionally if it exists but only checks for comment‑only or incorrect lines; it does not detect or preserve multiple umask entries that might intentionally exist.
The check for existing umask lines in /etc/profile uses a grep that may match lines containing a comment with the word 'umask' (though unlikely due to anchor).
Portraying new file creation without checking for pre‑existing custom umask scripts may override intended logic, but this is acceptable in most environments.
suggested_improvements:
Adjust the sed pattern to preserve the exact leading whitespace: `sed -i -E 's/^([[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+)[0-9]{3}/\1 022/' /etc/profile`.
Add a check to skip modifying /etc/profile.d/umask.sh if the file already contains a valid `umask 022` line to avoid unnecessary writes.
When writing the new /etc/profile.d/umask.sh, explicitly set the ownership to root:root to match system defaults.
Log each modification for auditability.
Optionally verify that `/etc/profile` uses `source /etc/profile.d/*.sh`; if not, add a comment explaining the dependency.
